title: ITSI Service Health Score Trending and Prolonged Degradation Detection (multi-window health-score regression, KPI-driven degradation attribution, episode-grouping correlation, dependency-tree fan-out, severity-blind backtest detection)
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.2.2 KPI degradation alerting focused on raw KPI threshold breaches, UC-13.2.10 KPI base search skip rate surveillance in isolation, generic Splunk Enterprise Security correlation except where NEAP objects are intentionally shared, or installation and licensing procedures for new ITSI instances.
evidence: Saved search uc_13_2_1_itsi_health_regression_neap_correlation with fifteen-minute schedule, exported CSV of alert rows joined to itsi_service_inventory.csv, and ticketed screenshots from Service Analyzer and Episode Review for the same service_id.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab ITSI tenant, tighten a non-production KPI threshold to pull Service Health Score under fifty-five for three consecutive fifteen-minute bins while NEAP rules remain loose, then confirm uc_13_2_1_itsi_health_regression_neap_correlation returns warn or page with severity_blind_floor equal to one and non-null recommended_action within thirty minutes.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After restoring thresholds and clearing synthetic load, when health_score averages exceed eighty, NEAP pressure is zero, and itsi_searchlog shows no skip bursts, filtering the saved search to alert_severity in page or warn should return zero rows for services under test.

dataSources: index=itsi_summary (KPI summary index where ITSI writes itsi_health_monitor and Service Health Score rows), index=itsi_grouped_alerts (Notable Event Aggregation Policy grouped notables and episode metadata), index=itsi_tracked_alerts (KPI severity transitions feeding scores), index=_internal sourcetype=itsi_searchlog (KPI base-search skip and error telemetry), REST GET /services/itoa_interface/service and /services/itoa_interface/notable_event_group on the search head for inventory freshness, lookup table itsi_service_inventory.csv keyed by service_id for cluster_tier, owner_team, and environment joins; optional index=itsi_metrics mstats lane when KPI metrics are mirrored to metrics indexes
spl:
`comment("UC-13.2.1 ITSI Service Health Score regression NEAP kpi_skip inventory join earliest=-4h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=itsi_summary earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,service_key,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | eval kpi_label=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kpi,kpi_name,""))))
      | eval health_score=tonumber(coalesce(health_score,itsi_health_score,score,null()))
      | where len(service_id)>2 AND (match(kpi_label,"servicehealthscore") OR match(lower(_raw),"servicehealthscore") OR like(sourcetype,"%itsi%summary%") OR source=="itsi_health_monitor")
      | eval service_title=trim(toString(coalesce(service_title,title,service_name,"")))
      | bin _time span=15m
      | stats avg(health_score) AS hs15 values(service_title) AS stv by service_id _time
      | eval service_title=coalesce(mvindex(mvdedup(stv),0),"unknown_title")
      | eval lane="health_summary"
      | fields _time service_id service_title hs15 lane ]
    [ search index=itsi_grouped_alerts earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | stats count AS neap_cnt values(severity) AS neap_sev by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="episodes"
      | fields service_id neap_cnt neap_sev _time lane ]
    [ search index=itsi_tracked_alerts earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | stats count AS tracked_cnt values(severity) AS kpi_sev_vals by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="kpi_severity"
      | fields service_id tracked_cnt kpi_sev_vals _time lane ]
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=itsi_searchlog earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | rex field=search "(?i)service_id\s*=\s*(?<service_id>[0-9a-f\-]{3,})"
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,"__global__")))
      | where like(_raw,"%skip%") OR like(_raw,"%error%") OR like(_raw,"%failed%")
      | stats count AS skip_cnt by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="kpi_skip"
      | fields service_id skip_cnt _time lane ]
    [ | inputlookup itsi_service_inventory.csv
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(service_id))
      | eval parent_service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(parent_service_id,parent_key,"")))
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="service_inventory"
      | fields service_id parent_service_id cluster_tier owner_team environment _time lane ]
| eventstats max(eval(if(lane=="episodes",neap_cnt,null()))) AS neap_episode_pressure max(eval(if(lane=="kpi_severity",tracked_cnt,null()))) AS kpi_severity_hits max(eval(if(lane=="kpi_skip",skip_cnt,null()))) AS kpi_skip_gap_hint max(eval(if(lane=="service_inventory",parent_service_id,null()))) AS dependency_context BY service_id
| where lane="health_summary"
| sort 0 service_id - _time
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=f avg(hs15) AS health_score_avg_15m BY service_id
| streamstats window=4 current=t global=f avg(hs15) AS health_score_avg_60m BY service_id
| eval health_score_avg_5m=hs15
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=f sum(eval(if(hs15<=60,1,0))) AS low_window_streak BY service_id
| eval prolonged_low_dwell=if(low_window_streak>=3,1,0)
| stats latest(_time) AS last_seen latest(hs15) AS health_score_latest latest(health_score_avg_5m) AS health_score_avg_5m latest(health_score_avg_15m) AS health_score_avg_15m latest(health_score_avg_60m) AS health_score_avg_60m latest(prolonged_low_dwell) AS prolonged_low_dwell max(neap_episode_pressure) AS neap_episode_pressure max(kpi_severity_hits) AS kpi_severity_hits max(kpi_skip_gap_hint) AS kpi_skip_gap_hint max(dependency_context) AS dependency_context latest(service_title) AS service_title BY service_id
| eval severity_blind_floor=if(health_score_latest<=56 AND health_score_latest>=44 AND coalesce(neap_episode_pressure,0)==0 AND coalesce(prolonged_low_dwell,0)==1,1,0)
| join type=left max=0 service_id
    [| inputlookup itsi_service_inventory.csv
     | eval service_id=trim(toString(service_id))
     | fields service_id cluster_tier owner_team environment ]
| eval cluster_tier=coalesce(cluster_tier,"unrated")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(owner_team,"unowned")
| eval environment=coalesce(environment,"unknown")
| eventstats median(health_score_avg_60m) AS env_median_hs BY environment
| eval alert_severity=case(isnull(health_score_avg_60m),"info", health_score_avg_60m<=40,"page", prolonged_low_dwell==1 AND health_score_avg_60m<=50,"page", severity_blind_floor==1,"warn", health_score_avg_60m<=60,"warn", true(),"info")
| eval recommended_action=case(severity_blind_floor==1,"tune_kpi_thresholds_ml_policies_and_neap_escalation_for_servicehealthscore_drill_service_analyzer", coalesce(kpi_skip_gap_hint,0)>0,"investigate_itsi_searchlog_skips_and_kpi_base_search_runtime_on_search_head", coalesce(neap_episode_pressure,0)>0,"triage_neap_episodes_correlate_tracked_alerts_and_dependency_parents", prolonged_low_dwell==1,"attribute_kpi_entity_rules_multiwindow_health_regression_compare_child_parent_rollups", true(),"continue_monitoring_service_health_dashboards_and_episode_queues")
| table service_id service_title health_score_avg_5m health_score_avg_15m health_score_avg_60m prolonged_low_dwell neap_episode_pressure kpi_skip_gap_hint severity_blind_floor alert_severity recommended_action dependency_context env_median_hs cluster_tier owner_team environment
description: Detects prolonged Splunk ITSI Service Health Score regression using fifteen-minute summary bins correlated to NEAP episode pressure, KPI tracked-alert attribution, KPI base-search skip telemetry, and service inventory enrichment, while flagging severity-blind floors near fifty without episodes.
value: Platform reliability teams shorten time to innocence when numeric service health decays slowly enough that classic episode rules stay quiet, while dependency rollups still threaten customer journeys. Episode managers gain evidence that NEAP behavior matches numeric floors, and executives see defensible tiered paging that demands dwell before interrupting nights and weekends.
implementation: Correlate index=itsi_summary Service Health Score lanes with NEAP pressure from itsi_grouped_alerts, KPI attribution from itsi_tracked_alerts, and skip telemetry from index=_internal sourcetype=itsi_searchlog; normalize service_id, apply multi-window dwell before pages, join itsi_service_inventory.csv for cluster_tier, owner_team, and environment; save uc_13_2_1_itsi_health_regression_neap_correlation on a fifteen-minute cadence; route page and warn rows through the platform queue with drill links to Service Analyzer, Episode Review, KPI threshold policies, and REST service inventory refresh.
visualization: Service Analyzer deep links, Episode Review triage columns, Glass Table health tiles with parent-child drilldowns, side-by-side tables of alert_severity and NEAP state, timeline of kpi_skip_gap_hint, and environment median overlays for env_median_hs.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(Application_State.state) AS app_state latest(Application_State.info) AS app_info FROM datamodel=Application_State WHERE nodename=Application_State earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Application_State.dest
| rename Application_State.dest AS service_key
| join type=left max=0 service_key [| tstats summariesonly=t latest(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS cpu_load latest(Performance.mem_used) AS mem_used FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
| rename Performance.host AS service_key ]
| table service_key app_state app_info cpu_load mem_used
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Application_State summaries for business services mapped into Application_State.dest when entity translate rules land KPI hosts there; accelerate Performance summaries for underlying hosts that back ITSI entities when you correlate infra saturation to health_score decay; retain at least thirty days for quarterly service reviews.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Service Analyzer and service views
Splunk Docs — Create KPIs and thresholding
Splunk Docs — Notable Event Aggregation Policy
Splunk Docs — Adaptive thresholding and ML
Splunk Docs — ITSI REST API reference
Splunk Lantern — operational best practices library

knownFalsePositives: Planned KPI threshold tightening during incident response can flash warn rows even when the service is stabilizing because old NEAP episodes remain open until analysts close them. Rolling search-head upgrades for ITSI reorder scheduler slots and can create short skip bursts in itsi_searchlog that resemble degradation in health_score averages until queues drain. Scheduled ML adaptive thresholding retrain windows widen bands temporarily and may suppress individual KPI severities while composite health_score still decays, so pair machine learning maintenance metadata with this alert. NEAP throttling and suppression rules that close episodes aggressively during storms can hide lingering fifty-point health floors unless you keep severity-blind detection enabled. KPI base-search skip events during nightly backup windows can create apparent recovery in averages when data simply stops arriving; always verify event continuity. Dependency-tree refactors that move parent_service_id values without refreshing lookups cause misrouted owner_team tags until configuration management catches up. Service-template re-links that drop Service Health Score KPI membership make health rows disappear entirely, which looks healthy but is actually observability debt. Glass-table rebuilds that change color thresholds without changing KPI truth create executive false alarms unrelated to Splunk alert_severity. Dashboard clones that apply ad hoc entity filters can disagree with NEAP grouping and cause apparent contradictions between Splunk tables and Episode Review. Sourcetype or compression changes on forwarding paths can hide KPI events while index volume looks stable, producing false calm until you inspect null KPI rates. Finally, itsi_summary ingestion lag during indexer maintenance delays health_score updates and can mask real improvement or real failure for minutes at a time.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: required — runtime service modeling, KPI engine, NEAP, summary indexes

detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with the reliability engineering leads who curate Splunk IT Service Intelligence services, the operators who maintain search heads that run KPI base searches, and the episode managers who tune Notable Event Aggregation Policy behavior in production. This use case is not an installation guide for ITSI; it assumes ITSI is already running and that you are monitoring the platform the same way you monitor any other business-critical application, using the artefacts ITSI writes into indexes, internal logs, and REST interfaces while real users depend on service models.

ITSI expresses service condition through KPIs, entity rules, service templates, dependency trees, and aggregation policies. A KPI can be driven by a scheduled base search that materializes per-entity metrics into the KPI summary pipeline, or by ad hoc KPI definitions that reuse saved workload without standing up a separate base search schedule. Service Health Score is a first-class signal that compresses many KPI severities into a zero-to-one-hundred health_score curve you can trend, compare across parents and children, and export to executive glass tables. Separately, ITSI severity labels such as info, normal, low, medium, high, and critical describe discrete threshold bands on individual KPIs and on aggregated episode objects. Teams routinely confuse the numeric health_score with textual severity; this control keeps both lenses visible so you can spot a service that sits at a numeric plateau while never crossing a severity ladder that would open a governed episode.

Dependency trees matter because parent health can rise when a child stabilizes, or fall when a child degrades, even when the parent KPI set is sparse. ITSI walks the dependency graph when it computes rollups, which means a prolonged dip in a leaf service can fan out through ancestors until an executive-facing parent shows yellow or red even though the originating defect is deep in the stack. Your Splunk search must therefore retain enough service identity to pivot from a parent row to child rows without forcing analysts to rebuild the tree by hand.

The itsi_summary index is the durable ledger where ITSI lands many summary artefacts, including rows associated with itsi_health_monitor style evaluations when your deployment emits Service Health Score KPI snapshots. Practitioners should validate the exact kpi naming string in their tenant; some teams mirror the string ServiceHealthScore while others rely on source keys that still map cleanly with a conservative match in SPL. NEAP groups related notables into episodes inside the itsi_grouped_alerts index so analysts see one queue object instead of dozens of chirping duplicates. Tracked alert history in itsi_tracked_alerts explains which KPI severities fed a score during a window, which matters when you attribute a regression.

Scope boundaries keep reviewers oriented. UC-13.2.2 focuses on KPI degradation alerting tied to threshold breaches on individual KPI metrics and correlation searches that fire when KPIs go unhealthy. UC-13.2.10 focuses on KPI base search skip rate and scheduler pressure on the search tier, which is an upstream cause of missing KPI rows rather than a story about multi-window health_score regression correlated to NEAP. This document centers on prolonged health_score erosion, multi-window averaging, NEAP correlation, dependency fan-out hints, and severity-blind floors where health_score decays toward fifty yet episode rules never engage because KPI thresholds or aggregation policies are too lenient.

Role based access is part of readiness. REST polling of /services/itoa_interface/service and /services/itoa_interface/notable_event_group typically requires itoa_admin on many estates, but some organizations grant a narrowly scoped itoa_user read entitlement for service inventory exports used in dashboards. Never embed high-privilege tokens in scheduled searches without vault rotation. Prefer workload identities tied to a service account whose only job is read-only ITSI object inspection.

Safety gates matter because ITSI is chatty during legitimate storms. Do not page on a single fifteen-minute dip unless your organizational culture explicitly wants that noise. Prefer dwell logic that demands multiple consecutive low windows before a page, and downgrade informational churn when maintenance metadata or change tickets indicate planned work. Severity-blind floors deserve explicit mention: when KPI critical thresholds are so loose that health_score can hover near fifty for hours while NEAP never opens an episode, your operators experience false calm. This control backtests the numeric floor against episode pressure so mis-tuned governance surfaces as warn rows even when the episode queue looks empty.

Finally, align visualization owners. Glass tables and Service Analyzer views are not data sources for this alert, but they are where executives confirm the story. Your Splunk rows should include recommended_action tokens that send humans to the same screens they already trust.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Treat ITSI telemetry like any other production data domain with five parallel lanes plus an optional metrics lane when your team forwards KPI measurements into a metrics index.

Lane health_summary targets index=itsi_summary and retains events that represent Service Health Score monitoring. Filter using kpi fields or raw text that includes ServiceHealthScore, and keep health_score, severity, service_id, and service_title on every row. Normalize time so fifteen-minute bins line up with NEAP windows you already use for change correlation. Retain parent keys when your summary stream already includes them; otherwise plan to enrich later with REST or lookups.

Lane episodes targets index=itsi_grouped_alerts for NEAP outputs. Persist episode identifiers, group severity, lifecycle state, and service identifiers that map to the same service_id keys you use in health_summary. If your NEAP emits nested JSON, flatten group_id and severity before indexing so SPL does not depend on fragile spath calls at search time.

Lane kpi_severity reads index=itsi_tracked_alerts for KPI-level severity transitions that explain why a health_score moved. Capture kpi identifiers, threshold labels, entity keys, and time. This lane is invaluable when you need to prove which KPI dragged the score even though the headline health_score average looked almost stable.

Lane kpi_skip mines index=_internal with sourcetype=itsi_searchlog for skip, error, and failure narratives involving KPI searches. These rows often include the search text that Splunk attempted to run for ITSI, which means you can sometimes recover service identifiers through disciplined rex extractions. When rex cannot find a service_id, bucket those rows under a sentinel key so the control still signals global scheduler pain.

Lane service_inventory uses lookup table itsi_service_inventory.csv keyed by service_id with columns for parent_service_id, cluster_tier, owner_team, and environment. Maintain this file through your configuration management system so tier and ownership survive reorganizations. Optional freshness can come from | rest /services/itoa_interface/service splunk_server=local when you want to reconcile titles and parent keys hourly; materialize REST results into a lookup if REST latency is too high for alert-time joins.

Optional lane metrics uses mstats against index=itsi_metrics only when your team forwards KPI numeric series into metrics indexes with predictable metric_name values. Disable this lane if you do not operate a clean metrics namespace for ITSI.

props.conf and transforms.conf guidance stays minimal here because this UC consumes ITSI-native indexes. Still verify TIMESTAMP_FIELDS on itsi_summary and itsi_grouped_alerts, and confirm _internal retention covers long holiday weekends when you will still want itsi_searchlog forensics. Legal teams occasionally care about service titles that include customer names; mask those fields at ingest if required.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as uc_13_2_1_itsi_health_regression_neap_correlation with a fifteen-minute schedule in steady state, dispatch earliest=-4h@h, dispatch latest=now, and throttle duplicate service_id rows for forty-five minutes unless alert_severity escalates from warn to page. Wire alert actions to the queue that owns ITSI content, not every downstream consumer team, unless cluster_tier equals a revenue-critical label in your lookup.

Pipeline narrative: multisearch fans health_summary, episodes, kpi_severity, kpi_skip, and service_inventory arms so one silent lane cannot hide a real degradation. eventstats lifts episode and skip pressure onto every health row per service_id before streamstats computes multi-window averages. The join to itsi_service_inventory.csv enforces cluster_tier, owner_team, and environment governance tags on the result. case() maps numeric regression and severity-blind floors into alert_severity, while recommended_action tells responders whether to open KPI threshold editors, NEAP policies, base search schedules, or dependency-tree comparisons first.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.2.1 ITSI Service Health Score regression NEAP kpi_skip inventory join earliest=-4h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=itsi_summary earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,service_key,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | eval kpi_label=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kpi,kpi_name,""))))
      | eval health_score=tonumber(coalesce(health_score,itsi_health_score,score,null()))
      | where len(service_id)>2 AND (match(kpi_label,"servicehealthscore") OR match(lower(_raw),"servicehealthscore") OR like(sourcetype,"%itsi%summary%") OR source=="itsi_health_monitor")
      | eval service_title=trim(toString(coalesce(service_title,title,service_name,"")))
      | bin _time span=15m
      | stats avg(health_score) AS hs15 values(service_title) AS stv by service_id _time
      | eval service_title=coalesce(mvindex(mvdedup(stv),0),"unknown_title")
      | eval lane="health_summary"
      | fields _time service_id service_title hs15 lane ]
    [ search index=itsi_grouped_alerts earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | stats count AS neap_cnt values(severity) AS neap_sev by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="episodes"
      | fields service_id neap_cnt neap_sev _time lane ]
    [ search index=itsi_tracked_alerts earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,itsi_service_id,"")))
      | stats count AS tracked_cnt values(severity) AS kpi_sev_vals by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="kpi_severity"
      | fields service_id tracked_cnt kpi_sev_vals _time lane ]
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=itsi_searchlog earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | rex field=search "(?i)service_id\s*=\s*(?<service_id>[0-9a-f\-]{3,})"
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(service_id,"__global__")))
      | where like(_raw,"%skip%") OR like(_raw,"%error%") OR like(_raw,"%failed%")
      | stats count AS skip_cnt by service_id
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="kpi_skip"
      | fields service_id skip_cnt _time lane ]
    [ | inputlookup itsi_service_inventory.csv
      | eval service_id=trim(toString(service_id))
      | eval parent_service_id=trim(toString(coalesce(parent_service_id,parent_key,"")))
      | eval _time=now()
      | eval lane="service_inventory"
      | fields service_id parent_service_id cluster_tier owner_team environment _time lane ]
| eventstats max(eval(if(lane=="episodes",neap_cnt,null()))) AS neap_episode_pressure max(eval(if(lane=="kpi_severity",tracked_cnt,null()))) AS kpi_severity_hits max(eval(if(lane=="kpi_skip",skip_cnt,null()))) AS kpi_skip_gap_hint max(eval(if(lane=="service_inventory",parent_service_id,null()))) AS dependency_context BY service_id
| where lane="health_summary"
| sort 0 service_id - _time
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=f avg(hs15) AS health_score_avg_15m BY service_id
| streamstats window=4 current=t global=f avg(hs15) AS health_score_avg_60m BY service_id
| eval health_score_avg_5m=hs15
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=f sum(eval(if(hs15<=60,1,0))) AS low_window_streak BY service_id
| eval prolonged_low_dwell=if(low_window_streak>=3,1,0)
| stats latest(_time) AS last_seen latest(hs15) AS health_score_latest latest(health_score_avg_5m) AS health_score_avg_5m latest(health_score_avg_15m) AS health_score_avg_15m latest(health_score_avg_60m) AS health_score_avg_60m latest(prolonged_low_dwell) AS prolonged_low_dwell max(neap_episode_pressure) AS neap_episode_pressure max(kpi_severity_hits) AS kpi_severity_hits max(kpi_skip_gap_hint) AS kpi_skip_gap_hint max(dependency_context) AS dependency_context latest(service_title) AS service_title BY service_id
| eval severity_blind_floor=if(health_score_latest<=56 AND health_score_latest>=44 AND coalesce(neap_episode_pressure,0)==0 AND coalesce(prolonged_low_dwell,0)==1,1,0)
| join type=left max=0 service_id
    [| inputlookup itsi_service_inventory.csv
     | eval service_id=trim(toString(service_id))
     | fields service_id cluster_tier owner_team environment ]
| eval cluster_tier=coalesce(cluster_tier,"unrated")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(owner_team,"unowned")
| eval environment=coalesce(environment,"unknown")
| eventstats median(health_score_avg_60m) AS env_median_hs BY environment
| eval alert_severity=case(isnull(health_score_avg_60m),"info", health_score_avg_60m<=40,"page", prolonged_low_dwell==1 AND health_score_avg_60m<=50,"page", severity_blind_floor==1,"warn", health_score_avg_60m<=60,"warn", true(),"info")
| eval recommended_action=case(severity_blind_floor==1,"tune_kpi_thresholds_ml_policies_and_neap_escalation_for_servicehealthscore_drill_service_analyzer", coalesce(kpi_skip_gap_hint,0)>0,"investigate_itsi_searchlog_skips_and_kpi_base_search_runtime_on_search_head", coalesce(neap_episode_pressure,0)>0,"triage_neap_episodes_correlate_tracked_alerts_and_dependency_parents", prolonged_low_dwell==1,"attribute_kpi_entity_rules_multiwindow_health_regression_compare_child_parent_rollups", true(),"continue_monitoring_service_health_dashboards_and_episode_queues")
| table service_id service_title health_score_avg_5m health_score_avg_15m health_score_avg_60m prolonged_low_dwell neap_episode_pressure kpi_skip_gap_hint severity_blind_floor alert_severity recommended_action dependency_context env_median_hs cluster_tier owner_team environment
```

Step 4 — Validate

Begin in a lab service that is not customer-visible. Induce a controlled KPI degradation by tightening a non-production threshold or injecting synthetic latency against a test entity rule, then confirm that health_score in index=itsi_summary moves in the same direction and timeframe as the Service Analyzer curve for the same service_id. Capture screen evidence from Service Analyzer and the Splunk row in one ticket so future reviewers trust the correlation.

Next, validate NEAP correlation by forcing a short burst of related notables that should group into one episode. Confirm itsi_grouped_alerts shows a single episode with rising episode pressure while health_summary still reflects the Service Health Score trend. Then relax NEAP throttling temporarily in a sandbox and confirm that the Splunk search still flags severity-blind floors when you artificially loosen KPI critical thresholds so health_score sits near fifty without opening episodes.

Exercise KPI base search skips by pausing a non-production base search schedule briefly, then restoring it. itsi_searchlog should show skip or error language, and kpi_skip_gap_hint should become non-zero for affected services. Confirm recommended_action steers operators toward scheduler inspection rather than blaming the network team without evidence.

Negative validation matters: after recovery, when health_score averages return above eighty and NEAP pressure falls to zero, the alert wrapper filtered to page and warn should yield no rows for services that are genuinely healthy. Compare dependency fan-out by degrading a child-only test service and confirming parent health_score movement appears in Splunk within the same window ITSI shows on the parent glass table.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case 1 — A revenue-critical service shows health_score pinned near fifty for six hours while Episode Review stays empty because KPI critical thresholds were widened during an incident; treat this as a severity-blind floor, open the KPI policy editor, replay tracked alerts, and tighten thresholds with change control rather than declaring victory.

Case 2 — Payment-service health_score falls and NEAP correctly groups dozens of notables into one high-severity episode; use the episode as the command center while Splunk rows confirm kpi_severity_hits climbed before the health average collapsed.

Case 3 — A child catalog service degrades and lifts a parent commerce service into warn without crossing paging thresholds; drill dependency_context and compare child rollups before paging the parent on-call.

Case 4 — KPI base search skip bursts in itsi_searchlog create a health_score gap that resembles recovery; confirm skip counts, restore scheduler capacity, and delay business communication until KPI rows are continuous again.

Case 5 — NEAP throttling during a planned change suppresses new episodes while health_score still decays; cross-reference change_ticket metadata and temporarily rely on warn-tier Splunk rows rather than expecting Episode Review to mirror every transient.

Case 6 — ML adaptive thresholding completes a long retrain window and temporarily widens bands; annotate the maintenance calendar so regression searches downgrade noise unless prolonged_low_dwell still fires across three windows.

Case 7 — Dependency-tree refactor day moves parent keys; refresh itsi_service_inventory.csv and REST exports before judging fan-out anomalies, or you will mis-attribute child signals to the wrong owner_team.

Case 8 — Service-template re-link changes KPI membership without changing titles; validate that ServiceHealthScore KPI still exists on rebuilt services and that kpi labels in itsi_summary still match SPL filters.

Case 9 — Glass-table rebuild swaps visual thresholds but not KPI truth; when executives panic over color while Splunk shows info severity, point to visualization drift and keep paging logic tied to alert_severity from this search.

Case 10 — Dashboard-driven false alerts appear when a clone of Service Analyzer applies ad hoc filters that disagree with NEAP scope; reconcile filter dimensions with episode policies before muting the platform alert.

Case 11 — Universal forwarder compression or mis-tuned props on a new intermediate tier drops KPI events silently; if health_score flatlines suspiciously while index rates look fine, inspect intermediate queues and prove events reached indexers.

Case 12 — False calm when itsi_summary indexing lags behind real time makes health_score look healthy; compare _time latency versus index=_internal metrics, then pause executive messaging until lag clears.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the rolled-up health number for important services across several time windows and compare it to open incident groups so we catch slow slides and blind spots where trouble hides just under the alarm line.
hardware: Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud search heads running ITSI with indexer clusters backing itsi_summary retention, supported on Linux hosts, often deployed on Kubernetes for cloud-native operator models.
